lesson.title: Earthing Systems and ADS Components
lesson.subject: C&G 2365
lesson.tonePrompt: Teach like a strong electrical tutor: practical, direct, technically precise, concise, and natural.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To identify the three primary earthing system arrangements (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and define the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
steps.retrievalText: In this lesson, we are looking at the safety systems that prevent electric shock. ADS (Automatic Disconnection of Supply) is the standard method used in the UK. You will learn to identify the three main earthing arrangements—TT, TN-S, and TN-C-S—and distinguish between the specific components like CPCs, Earthing Conductors, and Bonding. Understanding these is the difference between a safe installation and a lethal one. We will work through each system type and then look at how they work together to trip a protective device during a fault.

steps.title: ADS Components: Protective Devices
steps.objective: Identify the role of protective devices within an ADS system.
steps.retrievalText: ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is a safety system designed to cut off power instantly during a fault. The system relies on two main parts: an earth path and a protective device. The protective device is the active component, such as a fuse or a circuit breaker (MCB). When a fault occurs, the earthing system allows a high surge of current to flow. This surge is what triggers the protective device to 'trip' or blow. Without a functioning protective device, the 'Automatic' part of ADS cannot happen. The earth wire provides the path, but the protective device performs the actual disconnection.

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation ADS stand for?
Which component in an ADS system acts as the active part that physically cuts off the power?
What does the earthing system provide to ensure the protective device can trip during a fault?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is the protective device unable to perform the 'Automatic' part of ADS if the earthing path is missing?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The protective device requires a high surge of current to trip; The earth path allows that high current to flow during a fault; Without the path the current remains too low to trigger the breaker
steps.hint: Think about which part of the system actually 'trips' or 'blows' to stop the flow of electricity.

steps.title: ADS Components: Bonding
steps.objective: Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.
steps.retrievalText: Bonding is used to connect conductive parts together to keep them at the same voltage, creating an 'equipotential zone.' Main Protective Bonding involves thick green and yellow conductors that connect metallic services, such as incoming copper water or gas pipes, to the Main Earth Terminal (MET). This ensures no dangerous voltage difference exists between pipes and other metal parts. Supplementary bonding is additional bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms, connecting local metal parts (like a towel rail and a radiator) to ensure they stay at the same potential.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the term for connecting conductive parts together to ensure they stay at the same voltage?
Which specific type of bonding connects incoming metallic gas or water pipes to the Main Earth Terminal?
What is the name of the safety zone created when all metal parts are connected to the same potential?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way the purpose of bonding differ from the purpose of earthing?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Earthing provides a return path for fault current to trip the breaker; Bonding connects parts together to prevent voltage differences between them
steps.hint: Focus on the difference between connecting a system to the ground versus connecting two metal pipes to each other.

steps.title: TT Earthing Systems
steps.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
steps.retrievalText: In a TT system, the first 'T' stands for Terra (Earth), meaning the source transformer is connected to the ground. The second 'T' means the consumer's installation is also connected directly to the ground via its own local connection. The defining feature of a TT system is the Earth Electrode (usually a rod driven into the soil). A TT arrangement does not use the supplier's cable sheath for the earth return path; it relies entirely on the mass of the earth (the soil) to carry fault current back to the transformer. This is common in rural locations.

basicQuestions.questionText: In the code TT, what does the first letter 'T' indicate about the source transformer?
What specific component is driven into the ground to provide the earth connection in a TT system?
What medium does a TT system use to carry fault current back to the transformer?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is a TT system often used in remote rural outbuildings?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The supplier cannot provide an earth terminal; It creates an independent earthing point using an electrode; It doesn't rely on the supplier's cable for the earth path
steps.hint: Look for the system that uses a physical rod in the ground instead of a wire from the supplier.

steps.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.
steps.retrievalText: TN systems use the supplier's cable for the earth return path. In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for Separate. The Neutral and Earth conductors are two distinct wires throughout the entire network. In a TN-C-S system, the 'C-S' stands for Combined-Separate. In the supplier's street cables, Neutral and Earth are combined into one single conductor, known as a PEN conductor (Protective Earth Neutral). Once the cable enters the property, these are separated into two distinct terminals. This system is also known as PME (Protective Multiple Earthing).

basicQuestions.questionText: In a TN-S earthing system, what does the letter 'S' stand for?
What is the common alternative name for a TN-C-S earthing arrangement?
In a TN-C-S system, what is the name of the conductor that combines Neutral and Earth in the supplier's cables?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Where exactly does the 'Combined' conductor in a TN-C-S system become 'Separate'?
deeperAnswerGuidance: At the intake of the property
At the cutout
Where the supply cable enters the building
steps.hint: Remember: 'S' is for Separate wires all the way, while 'C' means they are Combined for part of the journey.

steps.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor
steps.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
steps.retrievalText: A CPC (Circuit Protective Conductor) is the earth wire for an individual circuit, such as the green/yellow wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable. The Earthing Conductor is the 'Master Earth Cable' for the entire building. It connects the Main Earth Terminal (MET) of the consumer unit to the supplier's earth point or the local earth electrode. The CPC protects its specific circuit, whereas the Earthing Conductor provides the final link that connects the whole installation to the ground source.

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation CPC stand for?
Which conductor connects the Main Earth Terminal (MET) to the supplier's earth point or electrode?
Which type of earth wire is found inside a standard 'twin and earth' cable for a single circuit?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is it technically incorrect to refer to a lighting circuit's earth wire as the 'Earthing Conductor'?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole installation; A lighting circuit wire only protects that specific circuit; The correct term for a circuit-level earth is CPC
steps.hint: Think about the scale: one is for a single light or socket, the other is the main link for the whole house.

steps.title: Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Systems and Components
steps.objective: Correctly identify a specific earthing arrangement and distinguish between its main components in a real-world scenario.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are inspecting a domestic property. At the intake, you see a lead-sheathed supply cable. A metal clamp is tightened around the lead sheath, and a 16mm² green/yellow cable runs from this clamp to the Main Earth Terminal (MET). Inside the consumer unit, you see a 1.5mm² green/yellow wire for the lighting circuit.
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the earth path is provided by the lead sheath and is separate from the neutral all the way to the transformer, this is a TN-S system.
Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² cable connecting the supplier's lead sheath to the MET is the Earthing Conductor.
Step 3: Identify the CPC. The 1.5mm² wire inside the lighting cable is the CPC.
Step 4: Identify the ADS Action. If a fault occurs, current travels down the CPC, through the MET, and out via the Earthing Conductor to the cable sheath, tripping the circuit breaker.
Final Result: TN-S system. Main link = Earthing Conductor. Circuit wire = CPC.
Takeaway: Apply the same identification method to the next case.
steps.hint: Focus on where the earth wire starts and what it is connected to: the supplier's cable sheath or an individual circuit.

steps.title: Guided Practice: Identifying TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Identify a TN-C-S earthing arrangement and its primary connection component based on supply characteristics.
steps.retrievalText: Scenario: You are at a modern house. The incoming supply cable has the neutral and earth conductors combined into a single PEN conductor. At the cutout, the supplier provides a terminal where the earth and neutral are separated. A 16mm² green/yellow cable connects this terminal to the MET.
steps.questionText: Using the scenario only, identify the single best earthing system type.
steps.hint: Combined then Separate = TN-C-S. The main link to the source is the Earthing Conductor.

steps.title: Independent Practice: TT Systems and Electrodes
steps.objective: Identify a TT earthing system and the specific component used to achieve a connection to earth.
steps.retrievalText: Scenario: You are working on a remote stable block. There is no earth terminal provided by the supplier. To protect the installation, a copper-clad rod has been driven into the soil outside. A green/yellow cable runs from the MET out to this rod.
steps.questionText: Using the scenario only, identify the single best earthing system type.
steps.hint: Think about the 'Terra-Terra' arrangement where the installation relies on its own local connection to the ground.

steps.title: Integrative Close: The ADS Chain
steps.objective: Synthesize the relationship between the earthing system type, specific conductors, and the protective device in achieving Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
steps.retrievalText: To achieve ADS, every component must work in a chain. When a fault occurs in a circuit, the current must have a clear path to flow back to the source to trigger the protective device. If any link—the CPC, the Earthing Conductor, or the Earthing System return path—is missing, the 'Automatic' disconnection will fail.
steps.questionText: State the main fault path in order from the appliance back to Earth.
answerGuidance: CPC to MET to Earthing Conductor; fault current path; protective device trips
steps.hint: Trace the current from the individual circuit wire, through the main link to the supplier, to the device that trips.

strengths: Uses realistic cable sizes (16mm vs 1.5mm) to help learners visualize the physical difference between components.
Focuses on the 'PEN' and 'cutout' keywords which are the primary identifiers for TN-C-S in exams.

phaseFeedback.phaseTitle: Independent Practice: TT Systems and Electrodes
strengths: The scenario of a 'remote stable block' is a classic, practical example of where a TT system is required.
Successfully links the individual components back to the overarching concept of the 'ADS Chain'.
issues: The answer guidance is contaminated with process descriptions rather than just the requested path components.
suggestedFixes: Restrict the answer guidance to the specific sequence of conductors/terminals.

candidateScore.summary: The lesson has a workable structure but still needs improvement before it clears the stricter dynamic quality gate.

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Critical rules:
- Terms must be lesson-specific and grounded in the source.
- Definitions must be short and technically defensible.
- anchorPhrases should be reusable phrases that later phases can teach verbatim or near-verbatim.

terms.simpleDefinition: A safety system that automatically cuts off power during a fault to prevent electric shock.
terms.anchor: The 'safety reflex' of an electrical installation.
terms.misconception: Thinking ADS is just a single component like a fuse, rather than a system of parts working together.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where the installation is connected to earth via a local electrode (rod) in the ground.
terms.anchor: Terra-Terra: Earth at the transformer and a separate Earth rod at the house.
terms.misconception: Believing TT systems use the supplier's cable sheath for the earth return path.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where the neutral and earth conductors are kept separate throughout the entire system.
terms.anchor: S for Separate: Two distinct wires for Neutral and Earth all the way back to the source.
terms.misconception: Confusing it with TN-C-S where the conductors are joined in the supply cable.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where neutral and earth are combined in the supply cable but separated inside the installation.
terms.anchor: C-S for Combined-Separate: Also known as PME (Protective Multiple Earthing).
terms.misconception: Thinking the earth and neutral stay combined once they reach the consumer unit.

terms.simpleDefinition: The specific wire that connects the metal parts of a single circuit to the main earth terminal.
terms.anchor: The 'earth wire' found inside a standard twin and earth cable.
terms.misconception: Calling the CPC the 'Earthing Conductor', which is actually the main link for the whole board.

terms.simpleDefinition: The main cable that connects the installation's Main Earth Terminal (MET) to the supplier's earth or an earth electrode.
terms.anchor: The 'Master Earth Cable' that links the whole house to the ground.
terms.misconception: Using this term to describe the small earth wires going to individual light switches.

terms.simpleDefinition: Cables used to connect metal pipes (gas, water) to the Main Earth Terminal to keep them at the same voltage.
terms.anchor: The thick green/yellow wires clamped to your incoming copper water or gas mains.
terms.misconception: Thinking bonding is the same as earthing; bonding prevents voltage differences between pipes, earthing carries fault current to ground.

terms.simpleDefinition: A metal rod or plate driven into the soil to provide a physical connection to the earth.
terms.anchor: The 'copper rod' in the ground used primarily for TT systems.
terms.misconception: Thinking every property requires its own earth rod; TN systems usually use the supplier's cable instead.

terms.simpleDefinition: A fuse or circuit breaker that 'trips' or blows to disconnect the circuit during a fault.
terms.anchor: The 'active switch' that performs the 'Disconnection' part of ADS.
terms.misconception: Thinking the earth wire alone stops the shock; the wire only provides the path so the breaker can trip.

anchorPhrases: TN-S has separate paths for Neutral and Earth.
CPC is for the circuit; Earthing Conductor is for the whole installation.
Bonding connects pipes; Earthing connects to the source.
misconceptionTargets: Interchanging the terms CPC and Earthing Conductor.
Assuming all earthing systems use a local earth rod.
Believing the earth wire itself disconnects the power without a fuse or breaker.

issues.problem: The planning phase could not extract enough concrete in-scope anchors from the source.
issues.suggestion: Strengthen the source text so the planner can identify multiple concrete teaching anchors.

teachChecks.title: ADS Components: Protective Devices
teachChecks.objective: Identify the role of protective devices within an ADS system.
retrievalTextLines: ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is not a single component, but a safety system designed to cut off power instantly during a fault to prevent electric shock. The system relies on two main parts working together: an earth path and a protective device.

The protective device is the active component, such as a fuse or a circuit breaker (MCB). When a fault occurs, the earthing system allows a high surge of current to flow. This surge is what triggers the protective device to 'trip' or blow. Without a functioning protective device, the 'Automatic' part of ADS cannot happen. The earth wire provides the path, but the protective device performs the actual disconnection of the electricity. You must remember that the wire itself does not stop the power; it only enables the breaker to do its job.
teachChecks.hint: Think about which part of the system actually 'trips' or 'blows' to stop the flow of electricity.

teachChecks.title: ADS Components: Bonding
teachChecks.objective: Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.
retrievalTextLines: Bonding is often confused with earthing, but they serve different purposes. While earthing provides a path for fault current to return to the source, bonding is used to connect conductive parts together to keep them at the same voltage. This creates an 'equipotential zone.'

Main Protective Bonding involves thick green and yellow conductors that connect metallic services, such as incoming copper water or gas pipes, to the Main Earth Terminal (MET). By connecting these pipes to the earth terminal, we ensure that if a fault occurs, there is no dangerous voltage difference between the pipes and other metal parts. Supplementary bonding is additional bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms. It connects local metal parts together, such as a metal towel rail and a radiator, to ensure they stay at the same potential, further reducing the risk of a shock if someone touches both at once.
teachChecks.hint: Focus on the difference between connecting a system to the ground versus connecting two metal pipes to each other.

teachChecks.title: TT Earthing Systems
teachChecks.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
retrievalTextLines: Earthing systems are identified by letter codes. In a TT system, the first 'T' stands for Terra (the French/Latin word for Earth), meaning the source transformer is connected to the ground. The second 'T' means the consumer's installation is also connected directly to the ground via its own local connection.

The defining feature of a TT system is the Earth Electrode. This is usually a copper-plated steel rod driven deep into the soil outside the property. Unlike other systems, a TT arrangement does not use the supplier's cable sheath or a shared neutral wire for the earth return path. Instead, it relies entirely on the mass of the earth (the soil) to carry fault current back to the transformer. This system is most common in rural locations or outbuildings where the electricity supplier cannot provide an earth terminal.
teachChecks.hint: Look for the system that uses a physical rod in the ground instead of a wire from the supplier.

teachChecks.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
teachChecks.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.
retrievalTextLines: TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth return path. The 'N' indicates that the installation is connected to the Neutral of the supply. There are two main types: TN-S and TN-C-S.

In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for Separate. This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are two completely distinct wires throughout the entire network, from the transformer all the way to the consumer's property. The earth is often provided by the lead sheath of the supply cable.

In a TN-C-S system, the 'C-S' stands for Combined-Separate. In the supplier's street cables, the Neutral and Earth are combined into one single conductor (known as a PEN conductor). However, once the cable enters the property, these are separated into two distinct terminals. This system is also known as PME, or Protective Multiple Earthing, and is the most common arrangement for modern UK homes.
teachChecks.hint: Remember: 'S' is for Separate wires all the way, while 'C' means they are Combined for part of the journey.

teachChecks.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor
teachChecks.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
retrievalTextLines: In an electrical installation, we use specific names for earth wires based on their location and job. A CPC, or Circuit Protective Conductor, is the earth wire for an individual circuit. For example, the small green and yellow wire inside a standard 'twin and earth' cable that goes to a single light switch or socket is a CPC.

The Earthing Conductor is the 'Master Earth Cable' for the entire building. It is the single, heavy-duty cable that connects the Main Earth Terminal (MET) of the consumer unit to the supplier's earth point or the local earth electrode. While both the CPC and the Earthing Conductor carry fault current, the CPC only protects its specific circuit, whereas the Earthing Conductor provides the final link that connects the whole installation to the ground. You must use these terms precisely: the CPC is for the circuit, and the Earthing Conductor is for the whole system.
teachChecks.hint: Think about the scale: one is for a single light or socket, the other is the main link for the whole house.

teachChecks.title: ADS Components: Protective Devices

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation ADS stand for?
Which component in an ADS system acts as the active part that physically cuts off the power?
What does the earthing system provide to ensure the protective device can trip during a fault?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is the protective device unable to perform the 'Automatic' part of ADS if the earthing path is missing?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The protective device requires a high surge of current to trip
The earth path allows that high current to flow during a fault
Without the path the current remains too low to trigger the breaker
The device remains closed and the fault persists
teachChecks.hint: Think about what triggers a circuit breaker to trip and what role the wire plays in that process.

teachChecks.title: ADS Components: Bonding

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the term for connecting conductive parts together to ensure they stay at the same voltage?
Which specific type of bonding connects incoming metallic gas or water pipes to the Main Earth Terminal?
What is the name of the safety zone created when all metal parts are connected to the same potential?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: How does the purpose of bonding differ from the purpose of earthing in a safety system?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Earthing provides a return path for fault current to the source
Bonding connects parts together to prevent voltage differences
Earthing aims to trip the breaker
Bonding aims to stop a person getting a shock between two metal objects
teachChecks.hint: One is about creating a path to ground; the other is about making sure two pipes are at the same voltage.

teachChecks.title: TT Earthing Systems

basicQuestions.questionText: In the code TT, what does the first letter 'T' indicate about the source transformer?
What specific component is driven into the ground to provide the earth connection in a TT system?
What medium does a TT system use to carry fault current back to the transformer?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is a TT system often the only option for a remote rural outbuilding where the supplier cannot provide an earth terminal?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The supplier's cable does not include an earth return path
A local connection to the ground is required for safety
The earth electrode creates an independent earthing point
It avoids the need for a long earth wire back to the main property
teachChecks.hint: Think about what the consumer must provide if the electricity company doesn't provide an earth wire.

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

{

    {
      "term": "ADS (Automatic Disconnection of Supply)",
      "simpleDefinition": "A safety system that automatically cuts off power during a fault to prevent electric shock.",

      "simpleDefinition": "An earthing arrangement where neutral and earth are combined in the supply cable but separated inside the installation.",

Explanation output:
{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "title": "ADS Components: Protective Devices",
      "objective": "Identify the role of protective devices within an ADS system.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is not a single component, but a safety system designed to cut off power instantly during a fault to prevent electric shock. The system relies on two main parts working together: an earth path and a protective device.",
        "The protective device is the active component, such as a fuse or a circuit breaker (MCB). When a fault occurs, the earthing system allows a high surge of current to flow. This surge is what triggers the protective device to 'trip' or blow. Without a functioning protective device, the 'Automatic' part of ADS cannot happen. The earth wire provides the path, but the protective device performs the actual disconnection of the electricity. You must remember that the wire itself does not stop the power; it only enables the breaker to do its job."
      ],
      "hint": "Think about which part of the system actually 'trips' or 'blows' to stop the flow of electricity."
    },
    {
      "title": "ADS Components: Bonding",
      "objective": "Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Bonding is often confused with earthing, but they serve different purposes. While earthing provides a path for fault current to return to the source, bonding is used to connect conductive parts together to keep them at the same voltage. This creates an 'equipotential zone.'",
        "Main Protective Bonding involves thick green and yellow conductors that connect metallic services, such as incoming copper water or gas pipes, to the Main Earth Terminal (MET). By connecting these pipes to the earth terminal, we ensure that if a fault occurs, there is no dangerous voltage difference between the pipes and other metal parts. Supplementary bonding is additional bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms. It connects local metal parts together, such as a metal towel rail and a radiator, to ensure they stay at the same potential, further reducing the risk of a shock if someone touches both at once."
      ],
      "hint": "Focus on the difference between connecting a system to the ground versus connecting two metal pipes to each other."
    },
    {
      "title": "TT Earthing Systems",
      "objective": "Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Earthing systems are identified by letter codes. In a TT system, the first 'T' stands for Terra (the French/Latin word for Earth), meaning the source transformer is connected to the ground. The second 'T' means the consumer's installation is also connected directly to the ground via its own local connection.",
        "The defining feature of a TT system is the Earth Electrode. This is usually a copper-plated steel rod driven deep into the soil outside the property. Unlike other systems, a TT arrangement does not use the supplier's cable sheath or a shared neutral wire for the earth return path. Instead, it relies entirely on the mass of the earth (the soil) to carry fault current back to the transformer. This system is most common in rural locations or outbuildings where the electricity supplier cannot provide an earth terminal."
      ],
      "hint": "Look for the system that uses a physical rod in the ground instead of a wire from the supplier."
    },
    {
      "title": "TN-S and TN-C-S Systems",
      "objective": "Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth return path. The 'N' indicates that the installation is connected to the Neutral of the supply. There are two main types: TN-S and TN-C-S.",
        "In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for Separate. This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are two completely distinct wires throughout the entire network, from the transformer all the way to the consumer's property. The earth is often provided by the lead sheath of the supply cable.",
        "In a TN-C-S system, the 'C-S' stands for Combined-Separate. In the supplier's street cables, the Neutral and Earth are combined into one single conductor (known as a PEN conductor). However, once the cable enters the property, these are separated into two distinct terminals. This system is also known as PME, or Protective Multiple Earthing, and is the most common arrangement for modern UK homes."
      ],
      "hint": "Remember: 'S' is for Separate wires all the way, while 'C' means they are Combined for part of the journey."
    },
    {
      "title": "CPC and Earthing Conductor",
      "objective": "Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In an electrical installation, we use specific names for earth wires based on their location and job. A CPC, or Circuit Protective Conductor, is the earth wire for an individual circuit. For example, the small green and yellow wire inside a standard 'twin and earth' cable that goes to a single light switch or socket is a CPC.",
        "The Earthing Conductor is the 'Master Earth Cable' for the entire building. It is the single, heavy-duty cable that connects the Main Earth Terminal (MET) of the consumer unit to the supplier's earth point or the local earth electrode. While both the CPC and the Earthing Conductor carry fault current, the CPC only protects its specific circuit, whereas the Earthing Conductor provides the final link that connects the whole installation to the ground. You must use these terms precisely: the CPC is for the circuit, and the Earthing Conductor is for the whole system."
      ],
      "hint": "Think about the scale: one is for a single light or socket, the other is the main link for the whole house."
    }
  ]
}

From the consumer unit, you see a 1.5mm² green/yellow wire emerging from a grey PVC cable that supplies the upstairs lighting circuit.",

parsed.title: Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Systems and Components
parsed.objective: Correctly identify a specific earthing arrangement and distinguish between its main components in a real-world scenario.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are inspecting a domestic property built in the 1970s. At the intake position, you observe a lead-sheathed incoming supply cable. A metal clamp is tightened around the lead sheath, and a 16mm² green/yellow cable runs from this clamp to the Main Earth Terminal (MET). From the consumer unit, you see a 1.5mm² green/yellow wire emerging from a grey PVC cable that supplies the upstairs lighting circuit.

Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Look at the source of the earth. Because the earth path is provided by the lead sheath of the supplier's cable and is kept entirely separate from the neutral conductor from the transformer to the property, this is a TN-S system (S for Separate).

Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² cable connecting the supplier's lead sheath (the earth source) to the MET is the 'Earthing Conductor'. It is the master link for the entire installation.
Step 3: Identify the CPC. The 1.5mm² wire inside the lighting cable is the 'Circuit Protective Conductor' (CPC). Its job is limited to providing a fault path for that specific lighting circuit only.

Step 4: Identify the ADS Action. If a fault occurs in a light fitting, the fault current travels down the CPC, through the MET, and out via the Earthing Conductor to the cable sheath. This high current flow causes the circuit breaker (the protective device) to trip automatically.
Final Result: The installation uses a TN-S earthing system. The main link to the source is the Earthing Conductor, and the internal circuit wire is the CPC.

Takeaway: Always use precise terminology. The 'Earthing Conductor' connects the whole house to the ground source, while the 'CPC' only protects an individual circuit. Mixing these terms up on a job or an exam shows a lack of technical understanding.
parsed.hint: Focus on where the earth wire starts and what it is connected to: the supplier's cable sheath or an individual circuit.

Grounded source text:
Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Grounded source excerpts:

Excerpt 1 (d9a6caef-6a5f-4977-9780-209ce55a5692): ## LO4 — Requirements for earthing systems **AC** 4.1 Identify earthing system types 4.2 Identify ADS component parts 4.3 Identify exposed conductive parts 4.4 Identify extraneous conductive parts 4.5 Identify components of an earth loop impedance path **Range** - Earthing systems: TT; TN-S; TN-C-S - ADS components: CPC; main protective bonding; supplementary bonding; earthing conductor; protective devices; earth electrode - Exposed conductive parts: steel conduit/trunking/tray; steel enclosures; metal accessories; metallic equipment - Extraneous conductive parts: metallic gas/oil/water pipes; steel ductwork; structural steel - Earth loop impedance path components: Zs; Ze; R1; R2; MET; supplier earth return path ---

      "whyItMatters": "Bonding is a critical safety component that prevents dangerous voltage differences between conductive parts.",
      "misconceptions": [
        "Confusing bonding with earthing; bonding connects parts together, earthing connects to the ground."

Vocabulary output:
{
  "terms": [
    {
      "term": "ADS (Automatic Disconnection of Supply)",
      "simpleDefinition": "A safety system that automatically cuts off power during a fault to prevent electric shock.",
      "anchor": "The 'safety reflex' of an electrical installation.",
      "misconception": "Thinking ADS is just a single component like a fuse, rather than a system of parts working together."
    },
    {
      "term": "TT System",
      "simpleDefinition": "An earthing arrangement where the installation is connected to earth via a local electrode (rod) in the ground.",
      "anchor": "Terra-Terra: Earth at the transformer and a separate Earth rod at the house.",
      "misconception": "Believing TT systems use the supplier's cable sheath for the earth return path."
    },
    {
      "term": "TN-S System",
      "simpleDefinition": "An earthing arrangement where the neutral and earth conductors are kept separate throughout the entire system.",
      "anchor": "S for Separate: Two distinct wires for Neutral and Earth all the way back to the source.",
      "misconception": "Confusing it with TN-C-S where the conductors are joined in the supply cable."
    },
    {
      "term": "TN-C-S System",
      "simpleDefinition": "An earthing arrangement where neutral and earth are combined in the supply cable but separated inside the installation.",
      "anchor": "C-S for Combined-Separate: Also known as PME (Protective Multiple Earthing).",
      "misconception": "Thinking the earth and neutral stay combined once they reach the consumer unit."
    },
    {
      "term": "CPC (Circuit Protective Conductor)",
      "simpleDefinition": "The specific wire that connects the metal parts of a single circuit to the main earth terminal.",
      "anchor": "The 'earth wire' found inside a standard twin and earth cable.",
      "misconception": "Calling the CPC the 'Earthing Conductor', which is actually the main link for the whole board."
    },
    {
      "term": "Earthing Conductor",
      "simpleDefinition": "The main cable that connects the installation's Main Earth Terminal (MET) to the supplier's earth or an earth electrode.",
      "anchor": "The 'Master Earth Cable' that links the whole house to the ground.",
      "misconception": "Using this term to describe the small earth wires going to individual light switches."
    },
    {
      "term": "Main Protective Bonding",
      "simpleDefinition": "Cables used to connect metal pipes (gas, water) to the Main Earth Terminal to keep them at the same voltage.",
      "anchor": "The thick green/yellow wires clamped to your incoming copper water or gas mains.",
      "misconception": "Thinking bonding is the same as earthing; bonding prevents voltage differences between pipes, earthing carries fault current to ground."
    },
    {
      "term": "Earth Electrode",
      "simpleDefinition": "A metal rod or plate driven into the soil to provide a physical connection to the earth.",
      "anchor": "The 'copper rod' in the ground used primarily for TT systems.",
      "misconception": "Thinking every property requires its own earth rod; TN systems usually use the supplier's cable instead."
    },
    {
      "term": "Protective Device",
      "simpleDefinition": "A fuse or circuit breaker that 'trips' or blows to disconnect the circuit during a fault.",
      "anchor": "The 'active switch' that performs the 'Disconnection' part of ADS.",
      "misconception": "Thinking the earth wire alone stops the shock; the wire only provides the path so the breaker can trip."
    }
  ],
  "anchorPhrases": [
    "TT uses a local rod.",
    "TN-S has separate paths for Neutral and Earth.",
    "TN-C-S combines then separates.",
    "CPC is for the circuit; Earthing Conductor is for the whole installation.",
    "Bonding connects pipes; Earthing connects to the source."
  ],
  "misconceptionTargets": [
    "Confusing bonding with earthing.",
    "Interchanging the terms CPC and Earthing Conductor.",
    "Assuming all earthing systems use a local earth rod.",
    "Believing the earth wire itself disconnects the power without a fuse or breaker."
  ]
}

Worked example output:
{
  "title": "Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Systems and Components",
  "objective": "Correctly identify a specific earthing arrangement and distinguish between its main components in a real-world scenario.",
  "retrievalTextLines": [
    "Scenario: You are inspecting a domestic property built in the 1970s. At the intake position, you observe a lead-sheathed incoming supply cable. A metal clamp is tightened around the lead sheath, and a 16mm² green/yellow cable runs from this clamp to the Main Earth Terminal (MET). From the consumer unit, you see a 1.5mm² green/yellow wire emerging from a grey PVC cable that supplies the upstairs lighting circuit.",
    "Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Look at the source of the earth. Because the earth path is provided by the lead sheath of the supplier's cable and is kept entirely separate from the neutral conductor from the transformer to the property, this is a TN-S system (S for Separate).",
    "Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² cable connecting the supplier's lead sheath (the earth source) to the MET is the 'Earthing Conductor'. It is the master link for the entire installation.",
    "Step 3: Identify the CPC. The 1.5mm² wire inside the lighting cable is the 'Circuit Protective Conductor' (CPC). Its job is limited to providing a fault path for that specific lighting circuit only.",
    "Step 4: Identify the ADS Action. If a fault occurs in a light fitting, the fault current travels down the CPC, through the MET, and out via the Earthing Conductor to the cable sheath. This high current flow causes the circuit breaker (the protective device) to trip automatically.",
    "Final Result: The installation uses a TN-S earthing system. The main link to the source is the Earthing Conductor, and the internal circuit wire is the CPC.",
    "Takeaway: Always use precise terminology. The 'Earthing Conductor' connects the whole house to the ground source, while the 'CPC' only protects an individual circuit. Mixing these terms up on a job or an exam shows a lack of technical understanding."
  ],
  "hint": "Focus on where the earth wire starts and what it is connected to: the supplier's cable sheath or an individual circuit."
}

Critical rules:
- guidedPractice retrieval and task must clearly mirror the worked example pattern.
- practice must be more independent than guidedPractice.
- Do not use these phases to smuggle in extra untaught chunk content that should have been its own teach/check section.
- Each phase must contain one coherent task with one response target type.
- Do not bundle several unrelated sub-questions, several scenarios, or mixed outputs into a single response field.
- Do not write identify-plus-explain bundles in one response box. If synthesis is needed, rewrite as one coherent explanation task.
- Do not ask for long ordered lists, “identify all of these”, or “answer 1, 2, 3” style tasks in these phases.
- If the learner must compare two items, the question must still produce one coherent explanation, not two separate hidden marking jobs.
- If a task currently asks for one conversion plus one classification, rewrite it so the learner gives one combined markable answer string or reduce it to one single target.
- answerGuidance must map directly to the exact response expected from that one task.
- If a task asks for a specific technical term or named item, answerGuidance must prioritise the exact target only.
- answerGuidance arrays must be concrete and gradeable.
- Never return empty questionText, empty retrievalTextLines, or empty answerGuidance for guidedPractice or practice.
- If you are unsure, generate a narrower single-target task instead of leaving any field blank.

{
  "guidedPractice": {
    "title": "Guided Practice: Identifying TN-C-S Systems",
    "objective": "Identify a TN-C-S earthing arrangement and its primary connection component based on supply characteristics.",
    "retrievalTextLines": [
      "Scenario: You are at a modern housing development. The incoming supply cable has the neutral and earth conductors combined into a single PEN (Protective Earth Neutral) conductor.",
      "At the cutout, the supplier provides a specific terminal where the earth and neutral are separated before entering the meter.",
      "A 16mm² green/yellow cable connects this supplier earth terminal to the property's Main Earth Terminal (MET).",
      "Inside the house, a 2.5mm² green/yellow wire is part of the ring final circuit cable."
    ],
    "questionText": "Based on the supply description where neutral and earth are combined in the street but separated at the intake, identify the earthing system type and the correct technical name for the 16mm² cable connecting the supplier's terminal to the MET.",
    "answerGuidance": [
      "TN-C-S and Earthing Conductor",
      "TNC-S and Earthing Conductor",
      "PME and Earthing Conductor"
    ],
    "hint": "Remember: 'C-S' stands for Combined then Separate. The cable linking the whole house to the source is the 'Earthing Conductor', not a CPC."
  },
  "practice": {
    "title": "Independent Practice: TT Systems and Electrodes",
    "objective": "Identify a TT earthing system and the specific component used to achieve a connection to earth.",
    "retrievalTextLines": [
      "Scenario: You are working on a remote stable block. There is no earth terminal provided by the electricity supplier.",
      "To protect the installation, a copper-clad rod has been driven into the soil outside the building.",
      "A green/yellow cable runs from the Main Earth Terminal inside the consumer unit out to this rod."
    ],
    "questionText": "Identify the earthing system used in this scenario and the specific technical name of the component driven into the soil to provide the earth connection.",
    "answerGuidance": [
      "TT and Earth Electrode",
      "TT system and Earth Electrode",
      "TT and ground rod",
      "TT and electrode"
    ],
    "hint": "Think about the 'Terra-Terra' arrangement where the installation relies on its own local connection to the ground rather than the supplier's cable."
  }
}

      "objective": "Identify the role of protective devices within an ADS system.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is not a single component, but a safety system designed to cut off power instantly during a fault to prevent electric shock. The system relies on two main parts working together: an earth path and a protective device.",
        "The protective device is the active component, such as a fuse or a circuit breaker (MCB). When a fault occurs, the earthing system allows a high surge of current to flow. This surge is what triggers the protective device to 'trip' or blow. Without a functioning protective device, the 'Automatic' part of ADS cannot happen. The earth wire provides the path, but the protective device performs the actual disconnection of the electricity. You must remember that the wire itself does not stop the power; it only enables the breaker to do its job."
      ],
      "hint": "Think about which part of the system actually 'trips' or 'blows' to stop the flow of electricity."
    },
    {
      "title": "ADS Components: Bonding",
      "objective": "Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Bonding is often confused with earthing, but they serve different purposes. While earthing provides a path for fault current to return to the source, bonding is used to connect conductive parts together to keep them at the same voltage. This creates an 'equipotential zone.'",
        "Main Protective Bonding involves thick green and yellow conductors that connect metallic services, such as incoming copper water or gas pipes, to the Main Earth Terminal (MET). By connecting these pipes to the earth terminal, we ensure that if a fault occurs, there is no dangerous voltage difference between the pipes and other metal parts. Supplementary bonding is additional bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms. It connects local metal parts together, such as a metal towel rail and a radiator, to ensure they stay at the same potential, further reducing the risk of a shock if someone touches both at once."
      ],
      "hint": "Focus on the difference between connecting a system to the ground versus connecting two metal pipes to each other."
    },
    {
      "title": "TT Earthing Systems",
      "objective": "Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Earthing systems are identified by letter codes. In a TT system, the first 'T' stands for Terra (the French/Latin word for Earth), meaning the source transformer is connected to the ground. The second 'T' means the consumer's installation is also connected directly to the ground via its own local connection.",
        "The defining feature of a TT system is the Earth Electrode. This is usually a copper-plated steel rod driven deep into the soil outside the property. Unlike other systems, a TT arrangement does not use the supplier's cable sheath or a shared neutral wire for the earth return path. Instead, it relies entirely on the mass of the earth (the soil) to carry fault current back to the transformer. This system is most common in rural locations or outbuildings where the electricity supplier cannot provide an earth terminal."
      ],
      "hint": "Look for the system that uses a physical rod in the ground instead of a wire from the supplier."
    },

        "In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for Separate. This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are two completely distinct wires throughout the entire network, from the transformer all the way to the consumer's property. The earth is often provided by the lead sheath of the supply cable.",
        "In a TN-C-S system, the 'C-S' stands for Combined-Separate. In the supplier's street cables, the Neutral and Earth are combined into one single conductor (known as a PEN conductor). However, once the cable enters the property, these are separated into two distinct terminals. This system is also known as PME, or Protective Multiple Earthing, and is the most common arrangement for modern UK homes."
      ],
      "hint": "Remember: 'S' is for Separate wires all the way, while 'C' means they are Combined for part of the journey."
    },
    {
      "title": "CPC and Earthing Conductor",
      "objective": "Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In an electrical installation, we use specific names for earth wires based on their location and job. A CPC, or Circuit Protective Conductor, is the earth wire for an individual circuit. For example, the small green and yellow wire inside a standard 'twin and earth' cable that goes to a single light switch or socket is a CPC.",
        "The Earthing Conductor is the 'Master Earth Cable' for the entire building. It is the single, heavy-duty cable that connects the Main Earth Terminal (MET) of the consumer unit to the supplier's earth point or the local earth electrode. While both the CPC and the Earthing Conductor carry fault current, the CPC only protects its specific circuit, whereas the Earthing Conductor provides the final link that connects the whole installation to the ground. You must use these terms precisely: the CPC is for the circuit, and the Earthing Conductor is for the whole system."
      ],
      "hint": "Think about the scale: one is for a single light or socket, the other is the main link for the whole house."
    }
  ]
}

Critical rules:
- Make the question synthesize the lesson ideas.
- Use one new applied scenario, not the exact worked-example scenario repeated.
- Prefer one integrative task with one coherent response target, not a loose list of mini-questions.
- Do not combine factual recall, list-making, and open explanation in the same response field.
- Do not ask for four or five separate identifications in one box.
- Do not write identify-plus-explain bundles in the integrative phase. Ask for one applied explanation that naturally includes the required relationship.
- If a draft integrative idea asks for both a system type and a component name, rewrite it as one explanation of why that component is correct for that system scenario.
- Do not introduce a new dimension, such as safety, cost, or installation method, unless that dimension was explicitly taught in the relevant chunk.

integrative.title: Integrative Close: The ADS Chain in a TN-C-S System
integrative.objective: Synthesize the relationship between the earthing system type, specific conductors, and the protective device in achieving Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
retrievalTextLines: To achieve ADS, every component must work in a perfect chain. The earthing system (TT, TN-S, or TN-C-S) defines the return path to the source, while the internal conductors (CPC and Earthing Conductor) provide the 'highway' for fault current within the building.

When a fault occurs, the goal is to create a current surge high enough to trigger the protective device (the fuse or circuit breaker). If the chain is broken—for example, if a CPC is missing or the Earthing Conductor is disconnected—the 'Automatic' part of the safety system fails, leaving metal parts live and dangerous.
integrative.questionText: A fault occurs on a metal light fitting in a house with a TN-C-S earthing system. Explain the path the fault current takes from that fitting to reach the supplier's network and how this path enables the 'Automatic' part of ADS. In your explanation, you must correctly identify the two specific types of earth conductors the current passes through.
answerGuidance: The fault current travels from the fitting through the CPC (Circuit Protective Conductor).
The current reaches the Main Earth Terminal (MET) and then travels through the Earthing Conductor to the supplier's intake.
In a TN-C-S system, the current then enters the supplier's combined neutral and earth (PEN) conductor.
This high flow of fault current causes the protective device (MCB or fuse) to trip or blow, which is the 'Automatic Disconnection' part of ADS.
integrative.hint: Trace the current from the individual circuit wire, through the main link to the supplier, and explain what that surge does to the circuit breaker.

parsed.title: Spaced Review: Fault Current Fundamentals
parsed.objective: Recall the relationship between fault current and protective device operation from Unit 203-3C.
retrievalTextLines: A 'fault to earth' occurs when a line conductor makes contact with an exposed conductive part (like a metal consumer unit or motor casing).
To trigger a protective device (fuse or MCB) instantly, we need a massive surge of current to flow through that fault path.
According to Ohm's Law (I = V/R), to achieve a high enough current (I) to trip the breaker, the resistance (R) of the return path must be extremely low.
parsed.questionText: If a fault occurs in a 230V circuit but the resistance of the path back to the transformer is too high, what happens to the 'Automatic Disconnection' part of the safety system?
answerGuidance: The high resistance will limit the fault current to a low level.
If the current is too low, it won't reach the 'tripping' threshold of the circuit breaker or fuse.
The metalwork will remain live at 230V, creating a lethal shock risk because the supply is not disconnected automatically.

lesson.title: Earthing Systems and ADS Components
lesson.subject: C&G 2365
lesson.tonePrompt: Teach like a strong electrical tutor: practical, direct, technically precise, concise, and natural.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To identify the three primary earthing system arrangements (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and define the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
steps.retrievalText:
Lesson: Earthing Systems and ADS Components
To identify the three primary earthing system arrangements (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and define the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
Why this matters: Without a functioning protective device, the 'Automatic' part of ADS cannot occur during a fault.
Core chunk map: ADS Components: Protective Devices; ADS Components: Bonding; TT Earthing Systems; TN-S and TN-C-S Systems.
By the end of this lesson you should be able to explain the main idea accurately and use it in one practical case.
We will work in short chunks: teaching first, then checks, then example, practice, and a final integrative task.

steps.title: ADS Components: Protective Devices
steps.objective: Identify the role of protective devices within an ADS system.
steps.retrievalText:
ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is not a single component, but a safety system designed to cut off power instantly during a fault to prevent electric shock. The system relies on two main parts working together: an earth path and a protective device.
The protective device is the active component, such as a fuse or a circuit breaker (MCB). When a fault occurs, the earthing system allows a high surge of current to flow. This surge is what triggers the protective device to 'trip' or blow. Without a functioning protective device, the 'Automatic' part of ADS cannot happen. The earth wire provides the path, but the protective device performs the actual disconnection of the electricity. You must remember that the wire itself does not stop the power; it only enables the breaker to do its job.

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation ADS stand for?
Which component in an ADS system acts as the active part that physically cuts off the power?
What does the earthing system provide to ensure the protective device can trip during a fault?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is the protective device unable to perform the 'Automatic' part of ADS if the earthing path is missing?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The protective device requires a high surge of current to trip; The earth path allows that high current to flow during a fault; Without the path the current remains too low to trigger the breaker; The device remains closed and the fault persists
steps.hint: Think about which part of the system actually 'trips' or 'blows' to stop the flow of electricity.

steps.title: ADS Components: Bonding
steps.objective: Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.
steps.retrievalText:
Bonding is often confused with earthing, but they serve different purposes. While earthing provides a path for fault current to return to the source, bonding is used to connect conductive parts together to keep them at the same voltage. This creates an 'equipotential zone.'
Main Protective Bonding involves thick green and yellow conductors that connect metallic services, such as incoming copper water or gas pipes, to the Main Earth Terminal (MET). By connecting these pipes to the earth terminal, we ensure that if a fault occurs, there is no dangerous voltage difference between the pipes and other metal parts. Supplementary bonding is additional bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms. It connects local metal parts together, such as a metal towel rail and a radiator, to ensure they stay at the same potential, further reducing the risk of a shock if someone touches both at once.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the term for connecting conductive parts together to ensure they stay at the same voltage?
Which specific type of bonding connects incoming metallic gas or water pipes to the Main Earth Terminal?
What is the name of the safety zone created when all metal parts are connected to the same potential?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way the purpose of bonding differ from the purpose of earthing in a safety system?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Earthing provides a return path for fault current to the source; Bonding connects parts together to prevent voltage differences; Earthing aims to trip the breaker; Bonding aims to stop a person getting a shock between two metal objects
steps.hint: Focus on the difference between connecting a system to the ground versus connecting two metal pipes to each other.

steps.title: TT Earthing Systems
steps.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
steps.retrievalText:
Earthing systems are identified by letter codes. In a TT system, the first 'T' stands for Terra (the French/Latin word for Earth), meaning the source transformer is connected to the ground. The second 'T' means the consumer's installation is also connected directly to the ground via its own local connection.
The defining feature of a TT system is the Earth Electrode. This is usually a copper-plated steel rod driven deep into the soil outside the property. Unlike other systems, a TT arrangement does not use the supplier's cable sheath or a shared neutral wire for the earth return path. Instead, it relies entirely on the mass of the earth (the soil) to carry fault current back to the transformer. This system is most common in rural locations or outbuildings where the electricity supplier cannot provide an earth terminal.

basicQuestions.questionText: In the code TT, what does the first letter 'T' indicate about the source transformer?
What specific component is driven into the ground to provide the earth connection in a TT system?
What medium does a TT system use to carry fault current back to the transformer?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is a TT system often the only option for a remote rural outbuilding where the supplier cannot provide an earth terminal?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The supplier's cable does not include an earth return path; A local connection to the ground is required for safety; The earth electrode creates an independent earthing point; It avoids the need for a long earth wire back to the main property
steps.hint: Look for the system that uses a physical rod in the ground instead of a wire from the supplier.

steps.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.
steps.retrievalText:
TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth return path. The 'N' indicates that the installation is connected to the Neutral of the supply. There are two main types: TN-S and TN-C-S.
In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for Separate. This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are two completely distinct wires throughout the entire network, from the transformer all the way to the consumer's property. The earth is often provided by the lead sheath of the supply cable.
In a TN-C-S system, the 'C-S' stands for Combined-Separate. In the supplier's street cables, the Neutral and Earth are combined into one single conductor (known as a PEN conductor). However, once the cable enters the property, these are separated into two distinct terminals. This system is also known as PME, or Protective Multiple Earthing, and is the most common arrangement for modern UK homes.

basicQuestions.questionText: In a TN-S earthing system, what does the letter 'S' stand for?
What is the common alternative name for a TN-C-S earthing arrangement?
In a TN-C-S system, what is the name of the conductor that combines Neutral and Earth in the supplier's cables?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the specific point in a TN-C-S system where the 'Combined' part of the system becomes 'Separate'?
deeperAnswerGuidance: At the intake of the property
Where the supply cable enters the building
At the consumer's terminals
The Neutral and Earth are split into two distinct paths at the cutout
steps.hint: Remember: 'S' is for Separate wires all the way, while 'C' means they are Combined for part of the journey.

steps.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor
steps.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
steps.retrievalText:
In an electrical installation, we use specific names for earth wires based on their location and job. A CPC, or Circuit Protective Conductor, is the earth wire for an individual circuit. For example, the small green and yellow wire inside a standard 'twin and earth' cable that goes to a single light switch or socket is a CPC.
The Earthing Conductor is the 'Master Earth Cable' for the entire building. It is the single, heavy-duty cable that connects the Main Earth Terminal (MET) of the consumer unit to the supplier's earth point or the local earth electrode. While both the CPC and the Earthing Conductor carry fault current, the CPC only protects its specific circuit, whereas the Earthing Conductor provides the final link that connects the whole installation to the ground. You must use these terms precisely: the CPC is for the circuit, and the Earthing Conductor is for the whole system.

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation CPC stand for?
Which conductor connects the Main Earth Terminal (MET) to the supplier's earth point or electrode?
Which type of earth wire is found inside a standard 'twin and earth' cable for a single circuit?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is it technically incorrect to refer to the green and yellow wire in a lighting circuit as the 'Earthing Conductor'?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole installation; A lighting circuit wire only protects that specific circuit; The correct term for a circuit-level earth is CPC; The Earthing Conductor connects the MET to the ground source
steps.hint: Think about the scale: one is for a single light or socket, the other is the main link for the whole house.

steps.title: Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Systems and Components
steps.objective: Correctly identify a specific earthing arrangement and distinguish between its main components in a real-world scenario.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are inspecting a domestic property built in the 1970s. At the intake position, you observe a lead-sheathed incoming supply cable. A metal clamp is tightened around the lead sheath, and a 16mm² green/yellow cable runs from this clamp to the Main Earth Terminal (MET). From the consumer unit, you see a 1.5mm² green/yellow wire emerging from a grey PVC cable that supplies the upstairs lighting circuit.
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Look at the source of the earth. Because the earth path is provided by the lead sheath of the supplier's cable and is kept entirely separate from the neutral conductor from the transformer to the property, this is a TN-S system (S for Separate).
Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² cable connecting the supplier's lead sheath (the earth source) to the MET is the 'Earthing Conductor'. It is the master link for the entire installation.
Step 3: Identify the CPC. The 1.5mm² wire inside the lighting cable is the 'Circuit Protective Conductor' (CPC). Its job is limited to providing a fault path for that specific lighting circuit only.
Step 4: Identify the ADS Action. If a fault occurs in a light fitting, the fault current travels down the CPC, through the MET, and out via the Earthing Conductor to the cable sheath. This high current flow causes the circuit breaker (the protective device) to trip automatically.
Final Result: The installation uses a TN-S earthing system. The main link to the source is the Earthing Conductor, and the internal circuit wire is the CPC.
Takeaway: Always use precise terminology. The 'Earthing Conductor' connects the whole house to the ground source, while the 'CPC' only protects an individual circuit. Mixing these terms up on a job or an exam shows a lack of technical understanding.
steps.hint: Focus on where the earth wire starts and what it is connected to: the supplier's cable sheath or an individual circuit.

steps.title: Guided Practice: Identifying TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Identify a TN-C-S earthing arrangement and its primary connection component based on supply characteristics.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are at a modern housing development. The incoming supply cable has the neutral and earth conductors combined into a single PEN (Protective Earth Neutral) conductor.
At the cutout, the supplier provides a specific terminal where the earth and neutral are separated before entering the meter.
A 16mm² green/yellow cable connects this supplier earth terminal to the property's Main Earth Terminal (MET).
Inside the house, a 2.5mm² green/yellow wire is part of the ring final circuit cable.
steps.questionText: Using the scenario only, identify the single best earthing system type.
steps.hint: Remember: 'C-S' stands for Combined then Separate. The cable linking the whole house to the source is the 'Earthing Conductor', not a CPC.

steps.title: Independent Practice: TT Systems and Electrodes
steps.objective: Identify a TT earthing system and the specific component used to achieve a connection to earth.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are working on a remote stable block. There is no earth terminal provided by the electricity supplier.
To protect the installation, a copper-clad rod has been driven into the soil outside the building.
A green/yellow cable runs from the Main Earth Terminal inside the consumer unit out to this rod.
steps.questionText: Using the scenario only, identify the single best earthing system type.
steps.hint: Think about the 'Terra-Terra' arrangement where the installation relies on its own local connection to the ground rather than the supplier's cable.

steps.title: Integrative Close: The ADS Chain in a TN-C-S System
steps.objective: Synthesize the relationship between the earthing system type, specific conductors, and the protective device in achieving Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
steps.retrievalText:
To achieve ADS, every component must work in a perfect chain. The earthing system (TT, TN-S, or TN-C-S) defines the return path to the source, while the internal conductors (CPC and Earthing Conductor) provide the 'highway' for fault current within the building.
When a fault occurs, the goal is to create a current surge high enough to trigger the protective device (the fuse or circuit breaker). If the chain is broken—for example, if a CPC is missing or the Earthing Conductor is disconnected—the 'Automatic' part of the safety system fails, leaving metal parts live and dangerous.
steps.questionText: Using the scenario only, identify the single best earthing system type.
steps.hint: Trace the current from the individual circuit wire, through the main link to the supplier, and explain what that surge does to the circuit breaker.

spacedReview.title: Spaced Review: Fault Current Fundamentals
spacedReview.objective: Recall the relationship between fault current and protective device operation from Unit 203-3C.
retrievalTextLines: A 'fault to earth' occurs when a line conductor makes contact with an exposed conductive part (like a metal consumer unit or motor casing).
To trigger a protective device (fuse or MCB) instantly, we need a massive surge of current to flow through that fault path.
According to Ohm's Law (I = V/R), to achieve a high enough current (I) to trip the breaker, the resistance (R) of the return path must be extremely low.
spacedReview.questionText: If a fault occurs in a 230V circuit but the resistance of the path back to the transformer is too high, what happens to the 'Automatic Disconnection' part of the safety system?
answerGuidance: The high resistance will limit the fault current to a low level.
If the current is too low, it won't reach the 'tripping' threshold of the circuit breaker or fuse.
The metalwork will remain live at 230V, creating a lethal shock risk because the supply is not disconnected automatically.

Grounded source text:
Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Grounded source excerpts:

Excerpt 1 (d9a6caef-6a5f-4977-9780-209ce55a5692): ## LO4 — Requirements for earthing systems **AC** 4.1 Identify earthing system types 4.2 Identify ADS component parts 4.3 Identify exposed conductive parts 4.4 Identify extraneous conductive parts 4.5 Identify components of an earth loop impedance path **Range** - Earthing systems: TT; TN-S; TN-C-S - ADS components: CPC; main protective bonding; supplementary bonding; earthing conductor; protective devices; earth electrode - Exposed conductive parts: steel conduit/trunking/tray; steel enclosures; metal accessories; metallic equipment - Extraneous conductive parts: metallic gas/oil/water pipes; steel ductwork; structural steel - Earth loop impedance path components: Zs; Ze; R1; R2; MET; supplier earth return path ---

Its job is limited to providing a fault path for that specific lighting circuit only.\nStep 4: Identify the ADS Action. The main link to the source is the Earthing Conductor, and the internal circuit wire is the CPC.\nTakeaway: Always use precise terminology.
